title: Insider Drops Mets-Clay Holmes Extension Update
description: The New York Mets are going to be sellers at the trade deadline and Clay Holmes could be on the move
fullText:
The New York Mets' postseason chances are near zero, and they could be the most active seller at this year's trade deadline.

They have plenty of rental arms teams could be interested in, including Clay Holmes. The Mets signed him to be a starter after he found success as a reliever with the New York Yankees. In his first year as a starter, he had a 3.53 ERA. This year, he had a 2.39 ERA through nine starts before landing on the injured list. He fractured his fibula after taking a combacker off the leg.

"At this point, an extension between right-hander Clay Holmes and the New York Mets ahead of the Aug. 3 trade deadline appears unlikely, multiple major-league sources say," MLB insider Robert Murray wrote on Thursday. "Things can change with one phone call, especially at this time of the year, so nothing can be totally ruled out. But Holmes is expected to draw significant trade interest in the coming days as he’s getting closer to returning from a fractured fibula that’s sidelined him since mid-May."

Holmes is expected to make his first rehab start on Thursday, and many teams will have scouts there to watch him. He has a $12 million option for next season, and considering how well he has performed as a starter, he could be interested in testing the open market.

The Mets would be wise to deal him for prospects in a lost season. He gets a ton of ground balls, and he could thrive on a team with an elite infield. The Milwaukee Brewers and Chicago Cubs would both make sense as landing spots for him.

title: Yankees Rotation Could Be Set Up To Carry Team To World Series
description: The New York Yankees Rotation is in a great spot
fullText:
The New York Yankees finally have Max Fried back, and their rotation is set up for a deep postseason run.

Between Cam Schlittler, Max Fried and Gerrit Cole, the Yankees could throw out an ace in every game if they roll with a three-man rotation in October. Will Warren or Ryan Weathers could serve as a fourth member if needed, but that top three is more than capable of getting the job done.

Schlittler is the favorite to win the American League Cy Young Award this year, and would likely start Game 1 of any series. Behind him is Cole, who has won a Cy Young Award and just delivered three straight quality starts. Finally, there is Max Fried, who, in his return from the injured list, went five innings, allowing just one hit and striking out seven batters.

Offense is still something the Yankees need to fix, but in October, pitching matters more, and the Yankees have a starting rotation capable of putting up zeros on a nightly basis. The bullpen is playing well right now as well.

David Bednar, Fernando Cruz, Brent Headrick and Paul Blackburn have all become weapons for Aaron Boone. Angel Chivilli has dominated in his nine outings this year as well. All of a sudden, the Yankees' pitching staff as a whole looks like their biggest asset.

When Aaron Judge and Giancarlo Stanton return, the Yankees will have a complete team, capable of winning a relatively weak American League.

title: Yankees Make Spencer Jones Roster Move Before Trade Deadline
description: The New York Yankees have quietly moved their top-ranked prospect outfielder following trade reports.
fullText:
The New York Yankees entered the final stretch before the MLB trade deadline with one of baseball's deepest farm systems and a front office facing difficult decisions. As the club continues weighing whether to move top prospects in pursuit of another postseason run, one of its most recognizable young players has remained at the center of speculation.

Hulking outfielder Spencer Jones has long been viewed as a cornerstone prospect thanks to his rare combination of size, athleticism and power. But after multiple trips between Triple-A and the majors this season, his future in the organization has become a popular topic as rival executives search for impact young talent and the Yankees look to upgrade other areas of the roster before the trade deadline.

FORBES | By Peter Chawaga
Cubs’ Craig Counsell Sends Front Office Message Shortly Before Trade Deadline

Industry chatter intensified this week when Jones' name surfaced in reports discussing the Yankees' deadline strategy.

"Spencer Jones and Jasson Dominguez are two of the key pieces the Yankees are planning to use as trade assets ahead of the Trade Deadline, according to industry sources,” MLB insider Francys Romero reported on X.

Whether New York ultimately parts with either of those promising young outfielders remains to be seen, but the report immediately fueled discussion surrounding Jones' place in the organization's long-term plans.

And that speculation gained another layer on Thursday, when the Yankees made another roster move involving the 24-year-old outfielder, moving him back down to their Triple-A affiliate just a few hours after calling him up to the big-league roster, according to the official transaction log.

FORBES | By Peter Chawaga
Trump Picks Replacement For Mets’ Steve Cohen Amid Historic Losing

Jones' latest demotion follows a very brief and unheralded opportunity to establish himself at the major league level, suggesting he might be falling out of favor in the Bronx.

“Spencer Jones made his big-league debut to great fanfare on May 8 in Milwaukee, having been called up from the minors when Jasson Dominguez landed on the injured list,” Erik Boland reported for Newsday. “Jones, considered a can't-miss Yankees prospect, much in the way Dominguez was at one point, was recalled to the majors for Wednesday's doubleheader against the (Pittsburgh) Pirates to significantly less fanfare. And this time around, his stay in the big leagues, his third stint this season, was assured to be a brief one.”

Indeed, the stint lasted less than 24 hours.

Through 31 major league games this season, Jones is batting just .224 with two home runs and a .669 OPS. His strikeout rate has remained a significant obstacle, with 35 strikeouts in only 76 at-bats.

For now, the Yankees appear focused on giving Jones additional at-bats in Triple-A while simultaneously evaluating their deadline options. Whether he returns to the Bronx later this season or becomes part of a blockbuster deal could be one of the organization's biggest storylines before the trade deadline passes.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

title: Mavericks match Knicks' offer sheet for Moussa Cisse, ink big man to two-year deal
description: New York forced Dallas' hand with an offer sheet and the Mavericks didn't wait long to make a decision.
fullText:
This story was originally published on July 18.

After a bit of uncertainty, Moussa Cisse is sticking around in Dallas for a while longer.

On Saturday morning, Cisse agreed to a two-year contract offer sheet with the NBA champion New York Knicks, meaning the Mavericks had 48 hours to match the Knicks' offer and keep the young center. Masai Ujiri and Dallas' front office didn't need the full 48 hours and matched New York's offer sheet on Saturday night, a person familiar with the situation confirmed to The Dallas Morning News. The team officially announced the move on Wednesday evening.

The Knicks' offer sheet to the restricted free agent was a two-year, $4.7 million deal with half the 2026-27 salary being guaranteed and the 2027-28 salary being non-guaranteed. The 6-foot-11 big man will now remain with the Mavericks on that same contract.

Cisse drew interest from New York thanks to his productive rookie season. While he only suited up in 38 games for the Mavs last season due to his two-way contract status, he averaged 4.5 points, 5.7 rebounds and 1.2 blocks in 13.9 minutes per game. Cisse flashed plenty of potential in that limited role, leading to outside interest that ultimately forced Dallas to match an offer sheet for the 23-year-old.

Instead of letting him get away to the Knicks, Mavericks will keep the talented young center as a piece in their rebuild around Cooper Flagg.

title: Mamdani sets internet ablaze with video explaining cave on plan to arrest Netanyahu: 'What a joke'
description: Zohran Mamdani admitted New York City cannot arrest Benjamin Netanyahu despite his campaign promises, drawing swift mockery from conservatives.
fullText:
New York City socialist mayor Zohran Mamdani was widely mocked by conservatives on social media Tuesday after announcing that one of his campaign platforms, arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, isn't a tool he has at his disposal.

In a video post on X that was viewed over 36 million times, Mamdani called Netanyahu a "war criminal" while conceding that New York City could not enforce the International Criminal Court's warrant for the Israeli leader's arrest on its own despite the mayor saying he had looked at exploring avenues to do so.

Mamdani has often said on the campaign trail and as mayor that he wanted to arrest Netanyahu over what the mayor calls a "genocide" in Gaza.

While Mamdani's post was well received by many on the political left, conservatives on social media quickly mocked the mayor over the situation while taking issue with his determination to enforce the ICC warrant, which the United States government does not recognize.

Mamdani's 'Deranged' Israel Take Resurfaces As He Targets Netanyahu

"Trying to find where Mamdani called for the arrest of the Hamas terrorists who plotted the October 7th attacks," Arkansas Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders posted on X. "Disgusting and Disgraceful."

"This may be the most horrific package of lies against the State of Israel ever promoted by a government official," former U.S. Ambassador to Israel David M. Friedman posted on X. "Even Hamas does not lie to this extent! It is despicable and entirely unbecoming a mayor of New York City. To those who voted for this Jew-hater, shame on you."

"Zohran Mamdani calling Benjamin Netanyahu a war criminal is utterly ridiculous," Sen. Tim Scott, R-S.C., posted on X. "The same crowd that rushes to condemn Israel never has much to say about the terrorists who started the war, massacred civilians, raped women, kidnapped children, and still hide behind their own people. Mamdani wants the headline that comes from attacking Netanyahu, not the consistency that comes with confronting Hamas."

Mamdani Calls Out Democratic Party As 'Well-paid Consultants And Big Money Interests'

"The mayor of New York is just straight up promoting antisemitic blood libel courtesy of Hamas propaganda from an official government social media account tonight," New York City Republican Councilwoman Vickie Paladino posted on X.

"As I was saying… he doesn't have the authority," US Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz posted on X. "More political theater."

"I had a dead homeless body in front of my apartment, but the mayor's focused on playing Model UN," Sam Raus, David Boaz Resident Writing Fellow at Young Voices, posted on X. "What a joke."

"Zohran Mamdani admitted tonight that he has zero legal right to arrest Netanyahu," political commentator Eyal Yakoby posted on X. "Like everything that he does, it was all just an act for clicks on social media."

Although Mamdani acknowledged he could not arrest Netanyahu as mayor, he called on the federal government to execute an International Criminal Court's arrest warrant if the Israeli leader visits New York.

"I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large," he said.

In a press conference on Wednesday, Mamdani answered more questions about his position and said, "my interest in executing that warrant is not specific to Benjamin Netanyahu, but to anyone who they put a warrant towards for war crimes and crimes against humanity."

Mamdani was asked at one point what he would say to those who are disappointed that Netanyahu can't be arrested by authorities in New York City.

"I'm simply saying that this is a city where, if anyone feels a level of frustration or opposition, protest is something that we will always respect," Mamdani said. "I leave it to New Yorkers themselves to make that decision and when it comes to Palestinian New Yorkers who are disappointed, I understand it because we are talking about someone who has been charged with crimes against humanity, someone who has visited this city on many an occasion and who has be welcomed by previous mayor and I want to make it very clear that that welcome ends with me."

Mamdani continued. "I will not be welcoming Benjamin Netanyahu or any other war criminals when they come to this city, and I do believe that our federal government should join the International Criminal Court and ensure that there is accountability when it comes to crimes against humanity."

Fox News Digital's Michael Sinkewicz contributed to this report.

title: New York City landlords warn American dream is slipping away under socialist rent policies
description: New York landlords push back against Democratic Socialists of America rent freeze calls, saying property taxes, insurance and mortgages erode profits.
fullText:
Democratic Socialists' calls to freeze rents and reject double-digit investment returns for landlords are fueling fears among some New York City property owners, who say years of rising costs and restrictive housing policies are pushing family-run businesses to the brink.

"It shows how flawed the system is in its entirety and how inefficient everything is here," New York landlord Jose Tur told Fox News Tuesday.

Tur, whose late grandfather spent decades building the family's New York real estate business after emigrating from Cuba, said the debate over landlord profits overlooks the hardships facing many small property owners.

Nyc Rent Board Dissenter Warns Mamdani-backed Freeze Could Hurt Affordable Housing Over Time: 'Slow Burn'

He recalled how his grandfather, who purchased his first apartment building in 1979, spent decades growing the family business before he was reportedly mugged at gunpoint by a tenant who had not paid rent in five years. Tur said his grandfather suffered a stroke and died two weeks later at age 99.

Fellow New York landlord Natalia Bonanno also relayed her family's struggle amid what she characterized as growing attacks from the far left: mounting operating costs that have made it increasingly difficult to keep rent-stabilized buildings financially viable.

She told "America's Newsroom" that her father came to the U.S. as an immigrant with a dream. He became the first in his family to earn a college degree and later became an engineer before deciding he wanted to own property.

"He started to invest also in the '70s and buying properties in Brooklyn and then passed them down to my sister and I," Bonanno recalled.

Today, Bonanno said that dream is becoming increasingly difficult to sustain. She disputed claims that landlords are routinely earning double-digit returns, arguing such figures ignore the rising costs of mortgages, insurance, taxes and the expense of maintaining aging rent-stabilized buildings, many of which were constructed in the early 20th century.

Steve Forbes: Mamdani's Socialist Rent-control Puts New York On The Road To Housing Ruin

"I don't know where they're getting 12% from," Bonanno said, responding to remarks made Friday by New York City Democratic Socialists of America co-Chair Gustavo Gordillo.

"I know a lot of people refer to the NOI figure that came out recently, and that doesn't take into account our mortgage and insurance," she added.

Gordillo, speaking to "The Story" host Martha MacCallum last week, argued that landlords should not expect double-digit investment returns and dismissed claims that rent freezes would drive property owners out of business.

Tur also disputed Gordillo's comments, arguing they were not made in reference to rent-stabilized properties, which he and Bonanno both own.

When asked whether they planned to remain in the business, Tur said he hopes to continue the work his grandfather spent decades building, describing it as a responsibility to both his family and the tenants he has known for years.

"I think it's the right thing to do on behalf of my family and what my grandfather built and also on behalf of the tenants," Tur said.

"These are people that we've known for several years. It's not like we're a huge company that's not present. We're boots on the ground."

Bonanno, however, said her family's future in New York is far less certain.

"We're selling most of our rent-stabilized buildings because we just don't see a future here for having profit," she said.

"We hope we could stay in New York City and maybe invest in non-rent-stabilized properties, but if not, we'll have to just sell and purchase properties in other states."

title: Zelle must face New York attorney general's lawsuit over fraud, judge rules
description: A judge rejected Zelle's bid to dismiss a lawsuit by New York Attorney General Letitia James, who said the electronic payment platform's refusal to
fullText:
NEW YORK, July 22 (Reuters) - A judge rejected Zelle's bid to dismiss a lawsuit by New York Attorney General Letitia James, who said the electronic payment ‌platform's refusal to adopt critical safety features enabled fraudsters to steal more than $1 billion ‌from consumers.

In a decision on Tuesday, Justice Phaedra Perry-Bond of a New York state court in Manhattan said James sufficiently alleged ​that Zelle's parent Early Warning Services "prioritized accessibility, convenience, consumer adoption, and market dominance at the expense of consumer safety" in rushing the platform to market, despite objections from its banking partners.

What role did banking partners play in Zelle's launch?

How much money did fraudsters steal through Zelle?

What safety features did Zelle allegedly fail to implement?

Why did the judge reject Zelle's dismissal bid?

Early Warning Services is owned by seven large U.S. banks: Bank of America, Capital One, JPMorgan Chase, PNC, Truist, US Bank ‌and Wells Fargo.

Perry-Bond said Zelle also conceded ⁠it is still collecting and retaining fees from fraudulent transactions, raising the question of whether it implicitly or expressly approved what the fraudsters were doing.

James also ⁠objected to how Zelle was marketed to consumers, including claims that the platform offered "peace-of-mind" and that Zelle was "backed by the banks, so you know it's secure."

"Reports of fraud and scams committed by bad ​actors ​against Zelle users have always been exceptionally low," spokesperson ​Eric Blankenbaker said. "The attorney general is targeting ‌our company for political gain by recycling claims that courts across the country have rejected as meritless. The attorney general's claims are not supported by either the facts or the law."

The attorney general's office was not immediately available for comment.

In seeking a dismissal, Zelle had argued it was not misleading to advertise the platform as safe and secure, and there was no liability for ‌what it called "passive nonfeasance" in allegedly creating an atmosphere conducive ​to fraud.

Zelle was launched in 2017, and competes with ​apps such as PayPal's Venmo and Block's ​Cash App.

James said typical scams involved hacking into users' accounts and making unauthorized ‌transfers, convincing users to send money for ​nonexistent goods and services, and ​impersonating banks, government offices and utilities.

She said it wasn't until 2023, after the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and several members of Congress began probes, that Zelle adopted "basic" safeguards it ​had proposed four years earlier.

James sued ‌after the CFPB dropped a similar case in March 2025. That agency ended most enforcement ​activity soon after U.S. President Donald Trump began his second White House term.

title: New York far-left group claims reparations 'owed' as Blacks' median wealth 'nearly 15 times less' than Whites
description: The NYCLU says Black New Yorkers hold nearly 15 times less median wealth than White residents, citing redlining and slavery's legacy in a new report.
fullText:
A prominent civil rights organization is pushing for statewide reparations, arguing that a historical debt is owed to Black New Yorkers due to deep wealth disparities rooted in the state's ties to slavery and decades of systemic discrimination.

"Reparations are a debt that is owed. Enslaved Black people built the foundation of New York's economy, but Black families in our state still face racism that has been codified into our laws and budgets," said Lanessa Owens-Chaplin, Director of the Racial Justice Center at the New York Civil Liberties Union (NYCLU). "New York State was not a bystander to slavery — it created, enacted, and protected the institution. Reparations must be extended to all Black New Yorkers descended from those enslaved by New York State, and to all Black New Yorkers still living under the systems slavery built."

In a joint report released last week titled "The Other New York: The Legacy of Slavery and the Case for Reparations Now," the NYCLU and the Legal Defense Fund (LDF) detailed the lasting economic impacts of slavery and Jim Crow–era policies on Black communities across the state.

Chicago Suburb Locals Hope Reparations Addresses 'Affordability Pressures' As Black Population Dwindles

According to the report, the median wealth of Black New Yorkers stands at $18,870 — nearly 15 times less than the median wealth of White New Yorkers, which sits at $276,900. The groups noted that New York state exhibits a wider racial wealth gap than the national average.

The report points to government-backed practices like redlining as major catalysts for today's wealth inequality, noting that the exclusion and displacement of Black communities denied them "the stability and generational wealth that homeownership provides."

"Nearly 90 years after redlining and urban renewal, formerly redlined communities remain some of the highest-poverty neighborhoods in New York," the report states. "Zoning laws reinforced the racial hierarchy established under slavery, forcing Black people to live in industrial areas with increased pollution — leading to disproportionately high asthma, cardiovascular disease, premature delivery, and premature death. Schools serving predominantly Black students have been severely underfunded and disproportionately affected by environmental hazards, crumbling infrastructure, and lead poisoning."

The groups argue that reparations must go beyond symbolic gestures, advocating for both direct compensation for financial losses and broad institutional policy changes.

"Supporting reparations is about more than compensation; it is about acknowledging harm, restoring dignity, and creating pathways for communities to thrive," the authors wrote. The report outlines proposed remedies to "compensate for the harm by addressing tangible losses, such as loss of wealth, wages, and other damages caused by wrongdoing," while also targeting broader structural changes in criminal justice, environmental policy, and public school funding.

"In order to build a thriving economy and a resilient multi-racial democracy, New York must repair past harm and break down barriers to opportunity for Black communities. Reparations benefit all New Yorkers," the report urged.

The NYCLU did not respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

Key Trump Agency Puts Top Universities In Crosshairs Over Alleged Race-based Dorm Programs

These localized efforts come as state and municipal racial redress initiatives face growing legal and political scrutiny nationwide. In Evanston, Illinois — the first U.S. municipality to distribute cash and housing grants as reparations — the program is facing a high-profile federal lawsuit. The U.S. Department of Justice intervened in the suit, arguing that race-based benefit distributions violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

Meanwhile, in New York, progress on state-level recommendations has stalled.

The New York State Community Commission on Reparations Remedies was created under legislation signed by Democratic Gov. Kathy Hochul in late 2023. The panel was tasked with examining the state's historical ties to slavery, subsequent Jim Crow practices, and modern economic disparities to formulate recommendations for policy changes and financial remedies.

However, the NYCLU slammed state officials after a provision quietly embedded in the state budget pushed the commission's final report deadline back two years — from its original 2025 date to 2029.

title: Jets' Geno Smith gets stern warning ahead of 2026 season
description: Geno Smith and the New York Jets have a quarterback situation.
fullText:
Jets' Geno Smith gets stern warning ahead of 2026 season originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

The New York Jets are in a bit of a pickle after their 2025 season, which saw the team with a 3-14 record. But, that doesn't mean all hope is lost. Head coach Aaron Glenn is working hard to get this team in better shape this September than it was last. Will it work? Time will tell.

A lot of the Jets' destiny is in their new quarterback, Geno Smith. Even though Smith hasn't been with the Jets since 2016, he still has a feel for how things in New York work, which should help.

But, ahead of the new season, Smith and the Jets are getting a warning of sorts. In a feature out on Monday, John Breech of CBS Sports names the Jets one of the worst quarterback situations in the NFL right now.

The Jets' quarterback room, including Smith, Cade Klubnik, Bailey Zappe and Brady Cook, is being called one of the worst by Breech. He points out the negatives of the situation.

In the piece, he says that, "Smith went from one of the most underrated quarterbacks in the NFL to one of the toughest to watch last season. The Jets also have fourth-round pick Cade Klubnik from Clemson, Brady Cook, who went 0-4 as a starter last season with two touchdowns and seven interceptions, and Western Kentucky legend Bailey Zappe."

So, it's not just Smith in question. The other quarterbacks are also under the microscope. Either way you slice it, the pressure is on Smith and everyone else in the Jets' camp to turn things around. That's the goal for Glenn this season, as lofty as it seems. Frankly, though, Smith and Glenn are both extremely talent in what they do, so don't count the Jets out.

fullText:
An autobiographical game about the devastating effects of chronic illness on an artist’s creative life gives players the chance to slow the pace right down

Bub does not look like a traditional video game. Artist and stop-motion animator Case Jernigan spends hours, days, months in his studio drawing, painting and delicately cutting out pieces of collected paper, while coder and gameplay designer Todd Anderson builds scripts and physics simulations to make them into a game.

In Bub, you play through the life of an artist in New York City, making art but also diving into his early memories and imaginative dreams. “You go from present day New York City into his childhood, or different sort of visions, which are all rendered in stop motion,” says Anderson. “His story is told in the present day, but also how he’s making sense of his past.”

Jernigan says Bub is based almost entirely on his own life, both as a child and an artist, and one of the biggest themes is health: “I realised that a lot of my anxiety I was experiencing, and other unusual symptoms like problems with my eyes and other senses weren’t just run of the mill panic attacks. When I got diagnosed with MS I had what’s called a relapse, a really bad one, and I got very sick and had a lot of problems with my hands and my ability to make art.”

Thus the tagline: the anxiety of “turning your memories into art while you still can”.

A decade ago, Jernigan had been working on a graphic novel of childhood anxiety and how it’s manifested into adulthood. But the format didn’t feel right; he wanted a way to have a bit more control over how a person was involved with the work, the pacing, the feeling of space. “What if you could choose exactly how long you want to be here, and whatever sensation you’re taking from it, whether it’s invigorating or calming or just beautiful,” he says. “You could sit there as long as you wanted.”

A core piece of the appeal of autobiography in games is in how the creator uses the mechanics to influence how the player feels. Anderson says he wanted to make sure that Bub’s controls and mechanics are intuitive but don’t always do exactly what you might expect: “That’s always the relationship between the player and the controls. It’s constantly changing throughout.”

So too with the visual style. The “New York City overworld” feels grounded with its consistent pen-and-ink style, coloured in with the different colours and textures of different papers Jernigan has collected over the years. “But then, as you get pulled into memories or through the process of art making for the character, you get into some of these more papercraft puppets, or the smudgy marker animation, or liquid and paint.”

The music works similarly, created by Adam Schatz of Landlady and Japanese Breakfast, and Old Feels’ Asher Kurtz. “These artists have contributed a great deal to the sensation that you feel when you play the game,” says Jernigan. “It’s wistful music, lots of drones, lots of layers and texture. We think the musical identity very much mimics the visual identity of the game.”

Everything is handmade. Currently the team is working on scenes in which the player will be able to take space to make art themselves, the sort of art that was created to make the game. “We want the player to really feel like an artist a little bit,” says Anderson, “And have some kind of undirected creative output.”

title: Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI race
description:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question
Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.
New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters
Trump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratorium

‘What’s the point?
fullText:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question

Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.

Google is struggling with the latest version of its Gemini model

 A Chinese company, Moonshot, put out a free, surprisingly capable model last week

 China’s new models yet again thrown the US dominance in AI into question

Google is months behind in its launch schedule for the new version of its flagship AI model, Gemini 3.5. According to Bloomberg, Google is attempting to improve Gemini’s coding abilities to catch up to Claude, the Anthropic bot that is widely considered the best AI coding tool. OpenAI released a new model at the start of July; Google had been expected to do the same in May. News of the delay sent Google’s shares sliding as investors questioned whether it could maintain its top position in the AI race.

A refreshed and newly capable Gemini might also have ameliorated the damage to the US’s reputation as the leader in AI this week – but didn’t.

As Google’s internal struggles became public, the Chinese startup Moonshot debuted an unexpectedly strong model, sending shivers through Silicon Valley and Washington DC alike. Moonshot’s Kimi K3 model featured surprisingly capable coding, particularly in a niche called “front-end coding”. “This may be the single biggest release of the year,” Anastasios Angelopoulos, the CEO of AI testing company Arena, said.

Unlike ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini, Kimi K3 is free to use and open-weight, meaning anyone can download it and run it locally on their own computers, as its core components are released to the public.

It’s been the general strategy of Chinese AI companies: open-source and open-weight models, beating US AI companies on price as Chinese manufacturers do, and mirroring the broader trade war of the US and China. DeepSeek inspired a major US market panic last year. Alibaba previewed its latest model, Qwen3.8 Max, on Sunday, showing off a similar number of parameters as Kimi K3. The company plans to publish the model’s weights soon, per Bloomberg.

The strategy has been made possible by Beijing, which subsidizes computing resources and power consumption for its domestic AI startups, allowing them to release their AI models for free. The basic logic goes that if these startups can’t compete on capability with US models, they can compete on cost and undercut the US parent companies, which need to earn a profit eventually.

It’s all causing major headaches in Silicon Valley. There are no longtime incumbents in the AI business to whom customers will default. If those buyers, especially businesses who need lots of generated code, can turn to cheap or free alternatives, there might be little need to spend big on brand-name bots. The South China Morning Post, which is itself owned by the Alibaba Group, reported last week: “As US AI costs soar, global businesses pivot to China’s low-cost, open-weight models.”

In the US, nerves are rattling. Hawkish Trump administration officials are rekindling their efforts to block any US access to Chinese AI models via de facto bans, commerce department rules or an executive order, Axios reported Monday.

OpenAI’s policy head for advanced AI, former Donald Trump adviser Dean Ball, wrote that Beijing’s open-weight approach to AI would lead to “full AI communism” with government-run datacenters powering AI as a public good. He denounced that future. OpenAI has been sounding the alarm about Chinese models for years, which reads as part political disagreement and part business strategy, since Chinese open-weight models threaten OpenAI’s projected profits.

“I would guess that the Trump Administration will at some point realize that their best strategy here would be to create large amounts of regulatory risk around the use of open-weight Chinese models,” Ball wrote.

There is no alignment between the US’s leading AI companies and the government players in the same industry. Emil Michael, the US Pentagon’s chief technology officer, responded to Ball: “Every industry/ecosystem has its supreme village idiot. Dean Ball is that for AI.”

As Beijing presents a unified front, the White House’s response is growing more scattered by the day. Trump himself had talked until recently of complete deregulation and never restraining any AI investment, research or releases. By contrast, the White House is restricting access – even allies’ access – to Anthropic and OpenAI’s advanced cybersecurity models. Even the personnel are changing. Chris Fall, the director of the Trump administration’s Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months into his tenure, Axios reported Monday.

Not all of the AI crowd is against these models’ incursion on US dominance. Bill Gurley, a longtime venture capitalist best known for his early backing of Uber, wrote of China’s AI models in the Washington Post: “They aren’t a security threat – they’re what competition looks like.”

China’s advanced AI does pose a risk to the global business of AI, which is, as yet, still a race without a clear winner. Cheap Chinese AI models may trump their expensive US counterparts. The entire US economy is tied tight to the AI industry, raising the stakes of the Chinese models’ capabilities. Undercutting the US’s AI frontrunners’ business could unsettle the entire US stock market even more so than it already has.

That competition is set to play out in the future. In the present, though, China’s recent releases have thrown the politics of AI in the US into chaos. That reads as the bigger win.

New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters

Trump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratorium

Albanese’s AI blueprint sparks calls for datacentre moratorium until new regulations in place

Employees are taking some of the US’s first collective actions to protect their jobs from AI

Last week, Meta workers filed a lawsuit against their former employer alleging that it automated their firing via algorithmic decision-making. Meanwhile, thousands of Google’s workers petitioned their CEO for layoff protections as the company shifts its spending from employees to AI infrastructure.

“Make no mistake: this is a company that is enjoying massive, unprecedented success,” Parul Koul, a Google software engineer and the president of the Alphabet Workers Union, said outside the company’s California headquarters. “These layoffs and cuts are not difficult decisions, but simply profit being put over the people that make this company run.”

These Silicon Valley employees are making these moves against the backdrop of the AI boom, which is changing software engineering rapidly and causing unions to gain popularity.

A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said: “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.”

Meta countered last week’s lawsuit with an unusually direct statement: “These claims lack merit and are not based on facts. Workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI.” Google did not issue a statement in response to last week’s petition.

We’ve seen Silicon Valley luminaries warn of futuristic apocalyptic scenarios, but we’re seeing those worries come to life among the fearful rank-and-file employees, who are now taking action. We’re witnessing how those workforces, who have a front-row seat to AI’s development, respond to its effects on their employment.

Read more from Reworked, a Guardian series about what’s at stake as AI disrupts our jobs.

‘Adversarial clothing’: are garments designed to confuse facial recognition systems about to go mainstream?

IBM loses quarter of its value as tech giant’s shares plunge and profits falter

‘What’s the point?’ Teenagers give their verdict on Britain’s social media curfew

Amazon Web Services customers receive bills for up to $1.5tn after global glitch

Trump made $1.4bn from crypto in one year. Is Justin Sun the man who helped him do it?

title: Yankees’ Expected To Sign Big Venezuela Catching Prospect Sebastian Perez
description: The New York Yankees are reportedly “set to sign Sebastian Perez for a $1.7 million signing bonus in the 2027 international drafting class,” states MLB International Reporter Wilber Sanchez, but Francys Romero was the first to report this news back in February. It was also mentioned by Sanchez in February that Perez originally had a […] The post Yankees’ Expected To Sign Big Venezuela Catching Prospect Sebastian Perez appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
The New York Yankees are reportedly “set to sign Sebastian Perez for a $1.7 million signing bonus in the 2027 international drafting class,” states MLB International Reporter Wilber Sanchez, but Francys Romero was the first to report this news back in February. It was also mentioned by Sanchez in February that Perez originally had a deal in place with the Pittsburgh Pirates, but that the deal fell through, also adding that some scouts believe “he’s one of the best catchers in his draft class” and has lots of power and speed that could translate well to the big leagues one day, reported Sanchez.

MLB International Reporter Francys Romero was the first to report that the Yankees were the “expected team to sign Perez” to this deal, with Perez being a catcher out of Venezuela. Romero further added that at this time, back in February, the signing period doesn’t open until January 15th, 2027, and that this is considered to be “the first major agreement since the Yankees found a new international director of scouting”, stated Romero. Romero also added how Perez’s “original deal with the Pirates was for $1.3 million, but Perez’s representatives went back out on the market for a larger bonus deal”, reported Romero.

Romero has also described Perez’s scouting report as similar to Sanchez’s report today, noting that he hits for a lot of power (high exit velocity) and is an “excellent” defender behind the plate. Reporter Wilber Sanchez recently interviewed Perez to gather his thoughts. When discussing what it means to join the Yankees organization, Perez stated, “I feel good, I’m so excited to belong to the Yankees.” Perez even went on to add how he loved this organization growing up, and his goal is to make the Major Leagues within the next couple of years, Perez said.

The Yankees have changed their approach to international scouting and signing in recent months. The Yankees organization had “parted ways” with former director of scouting Danny Rowland, who had been the Yankees’ head director of international scouting for 15 seasons. Rowland signed international players such as Jasson Dominguez, Miguel Andujar, Luis Severino, and Roderick Arias, but there wasn’t a good track record of success in this department.

The Yankees felt it was best to go in a different direction, leading them to promote Mario Garza as the new director of international scouting this past offseason, a move that was officially announced on June 28th via a report from Yankees beat writer Bryan Hoch of MLB.com. Garza has been with the Yankees for up to 16 years and has reportedly worked in a variety of player development and coaching positions with the Yankees in their farm system, reported Hoch. The Yankees have taken a liking to Garza and his strategy as it relates to evaluating talent, as Hoch also reported Yankees General Manager Brian Cashman, when speaking about Garza, stated: “He knows our systems. He already knows all the personnel we want”, Cashman told Hoch.

The Yankees International Scouting Department has gotten to work recently. Not just with this reported deal expected in January, but also with the recent signing of Taiwanese pitcher Chien Fan Lai, who is fresh out of high school, has already reported to the Yankees Dominican Summer League Academy, and is currently in a throwing program. The Yankees’ overhaul in their international scouting department is a change they hope pays dividends 10-15 years down the line as new talent enters the Bronx in the foreseeable future.

title: ‘It’s About Showing Respect’ — Ex-Ravens DB Sends Stern Message To Jaxson Dart Amid Rising Concerns
description: Last year, when rookie Jaxson Dart made his New York Giants debut, he breathed life into a fanbase in desperate need of a pick-me-up. The 25th overall pick in the first round of the 2025 NFL Draft flashed true playmaking ability.
fullText:
Last year, when rookie Jaxson Dart made his New York Giants debut, he breathed life into a fanbase in desperate need of a pick-me-up. The 25th overall pick in the first round of the 2025 NFL Draft flashed true playmaking ability.

Yet, at times, a reckless play style led to turmoil. For Dart, it meant dealing with concussion symptoms that knocked him out of one game and kept him out of the next two. Now one former NFL defensive back has a message for the Giants’ 23-year-old franchise quarterback.

Over the weekend, Giants running back Cam Skattebo drew criticism at the Fanatics Fest for a failed backflip attempt. The frustrations didn’t stem from the fact that he didn’t complete the acrobatic move successfully; rather, it was because he’s pulling risky stunts away from the NFL field.

That sparked a conversation on ESPN’s “Get Up” program, to which former NFLPA president and current ESPN NFL analyst Domonique Foxworth had some advice for Dart. He says the young QB needs to make better “business decisions” out of respect for his teammates.

But that doesn’t mean eliminating Dart’s trademark aggressive nature on the field that fans have come to love him for.

“I’d be much more concerned about it from a quarterback standpoint than from the running back’s standpoint,” Foxworth said.

“You have to get to a point where Dart understands that it’s not only putting yourself at risk. It’s about showing respect to everybody else on this team.”

BUILD YOUR OWN TOP 100: PFN’s FREE NFL Top 100 Builder

So, how does Dart do that? Foxworth suggests Dart would be better off practicing the mantra of living to see another down. Take what the defense gives you, and learn to trust your teammates.

But Foxworth adds that Dart doesn’t have to change who he is as a player; he can still make plays with his feet, he just needs to be smarter.

BE THE GM OF YOUR FAVORITE TEAM: PFN’s FREE NFL Mock Draft Simulator

“You don’t have to win every play,” Foxworth stressed. “You don’t have to win every single time you touch the ball. There are other people who can make plays, and nobody eats if you’re hurt.

“That’s the responsibility part. The aggressiveness, the linebacker nature of his mindset? That’s not the problem. You gotta be able to make a smart business decision. That’s the challenge, and I think he’ll get there.”

Foxworth is a former defensive back who played seven NFL seasons, including with the Baltimore Ravens from 2009 to 2011 until knee problems ended his career. Now, the former Harvard grad is offering his wisdom to Dart, and he believes the Giants QB will figure it out.

After all, Dart was just a rookie last season. He was still getting used to the size, strength, and speed of the NFL.

It usually takes young players a few seasons to get fully adjusted to making the jump from college to the pros, and Dart had his fair share of learning moments last season. Whether he learns from them and shows “respect” to his teammates by making smarter decisions remains to be seen.

title: Is Paige Bueckers playing tonight? Latest injury update for Wings vs. Liberty
description: Here's the latest injury update on Paige Bueckers ahead of the Dallas Wings game against the New York Liberty on Monday.
fullText:
The Dallas Wings had quite the injury scare on Sunday night with star guard Paige Bueckers.

Bueckers exited the July 19 contest against the Los Angeles Sparks early after she appeared to hit the back of her head on the court after Sparks forward Nneka Ogwumike accidentally landed on Bueckers after being fouled.

The initial fear was that Bueckers had suffered a concussion, which left her status up in the air before evaluations could be done.

The Wings have since provided an update on Bueckers that contains more good news than bad ahead of the Monday night contest against the New York Liberty.

MORE: Sophie Cunningham shows no mercy at youth basketball camp

The Wings announced that Paige Bueckers will not play against the Liberty on Monday night.

Thankfully, Bueckers has been cleared of a concussion, but she is still feeling soreness and the Wings are erring on the side of caution, as they should with their best player.

“Paige Bueckers will not play in tonight’s game against the New York Liberty,” the Wings said in a statement. “While Bueckers was cleared from having a concussion following an extensive post-game evaluation, and is not currently in the League’s concussion protocol, she will miss tonight’s game while dealing with soreness.”

MORE: Caitlin Clark has one-word hot take on World Cup final

After their meeting with the Liberty, the Wings have one more game on July 22 before the All-Star break.

It remains to be seen if Bueckers will suit up for that contest against the Portland Fire and, as of now, her status is up in the air for the WNBA All-Star Game, too.

That said, considering the fact that she’s just dealing with soreness, Bueckers probably has a better chance than not to play in the All-Star Game on July 25.

There is no replacing Paige Bueckers and her absence is a massive blow to the Wings’ chances of beating the Liberty.

Not only is Bueckers the Wings’ best player, she’s one of the best players in the league.

Bueckers is a dynamic scorer, as evidenced by her 20.9 points per contest, which ranks fourth in the WNBA, and she’s an elite distributor and a top-notch rebounder for a guard.

MORE: Tempo’s Sandy Brondello suspended for Angel Reese comment

Buckers’ 6.2 assists per game ranks sixth in the league, and her 4.2 rebounds per is seventh among all guards.

The Wings are going to need significant contributions from multiple players in order to even come close to filling Bueckers’ shoes.

title: Woke language overhaul would strip the word 'inmate' from New York City's entire jail rulebook
description: The New York City Board of Correction is replacing "inmate" with "person in custody" across jail regulations as part of a person-first language effort.
fullText:
The New York City Board of Correction is moving to scrub the word "inmate" from its jail regulations, replacing it with "person in custody" as part of a years-long effort rooted in the belief that incarceration should not define a person.

The latest proposal builds on a 2021 initiative that began removing the term "inmate" from the board's Minimum Standards after officials concluded people should not be defined by their incarceration.

"People in DOC custody are people first and the circumstance of their incarceration is not their defining feature," the board wrote in 2021. "Therefore, the Board has made a commitment to employ person-first language in its Standards and general communications going forward."

Mamdani Sparks Fierce Backlash For World Cup Stunt 'Palling Around' With Rikers Island Convicts

As part of that commitment, the board began replacing references to "inmates" with terms including "people," "persons" and "individuals in custody." The latest proposal would extend those revisions to additional portions of the regulations.

The changes are reflected throughout the rulebook.

A section formerly titled "Classification of Prisoners," for example, was renamed "Classification of People in Custody," while numerous references to "prisoners" and "inmates" were rewritten. The regulations also define a "person in custody" as "any person confined in a facility."

The proposal says the amendments are intended to continue the board's earlier work.

"In 2021, the Board made a commitment to employ person-first language in its Minimum Standards and communications by deleting references to 'inmates' in favor of person-first terms such as 'people in custody,'" the proposal states. "The Board proposes continuing this vital effort in Chapters 2 and 3."

Follow The Fox True Crime Team On X

The city is not alone in moving away from the word "inmate."

Like What You're Reading? Click Here For More

In 2022, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed legislation replacing references to "inmate" throughout state law with "incarcerated individual."

The measure amended dozens of provisions across New York law to adopt the new terminology, though New York City instead chose the phrase "person in custody."

The terminology debate has resurfaced as New York City's correction system has drawn renewed public attention following a FIFA World Cup semifinal watch party at Rikers Island attended by Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

During the event, Mamdani told attendees, "These are New Yorkers, and they will be New Yorkers when they get out of Rikers."

The city's terminology changes do not alter jail operations, housing policies or disciplinary procedures.

Instead, the board has characterized the revisions as an effort to modernize its regulations and ensure the language reflects its long-standing philosophy that people should not be defined solely by their incarceration.

Fox News Digital reached out to the New York City Board of Correction asking why it chose "person in custody" instead of "incarcerated individual," the term adopted by New York state, and whether the board received public feedback on the proposal.

Requests for comment were also sent to the Correction Officers' Benevolent Association, which represents New York City's correction officers, and the Vera Institute of Justice, a nonprofit criminal justice reform organization.

title: Tom Homan warns ICE surge coming to New York City as Mamdani boasts about sanctuary status
description: Tom Homan warns of an ICE surge in New York City, firing back at Mayor Zohran Mamdani's sanctuary city stance and vowing deportation efforts.
fullText:
White House border czar Tom Homan warned of an incoming ICE surge in New York City, citing the Trump administration's refusal to be deterred by Mayor Zohran Mamdani's embrace of the Big Apple's sanctuary status.

"It's coming," Homan told the hosts of "Fox & Friends Weekend" on Sunday.

While remaining tight-lipped on further details, Homan clarified his reluctance to elaborate stems from a refusal to show the Trump administration's hand.

Gop Senators Rally Around New Dhs Proposal Targeting Sanctuary Cities: 'Should Pay A Price'

"ICE is operating in New York City today. We're operating across the state today, but there's going to be a surge, and there needs to be a surge," he said.

"But I am not going to show our hand because there are... well-funded groups who will try to impede, interfere. We're not going to give them any upside on this," he added.

Homan's remarks came after Mamdani expressed "pride" in the city's sanctuary identity and asserted its unwillingness to participate in immigration enforcement during a New York Times interview Saturday.

Fetterman Warns Mamdani Risks 'Constitutional Crisis' By Vowing To Defy Scotus Immigration Ruling

"What we are unwilling to do is to participate in civil immigration enforcement with a federal government that has said openly it wants to deport a vast majority of people for crimes that we will never even know," he said.

When asked by The Times if he had been to the U.S.-Mexico border as a follow-up, he replied, "No."

Homan fired back at the sound-bite from the interview, contending that the democratic socialist can have his stance, but he will not prevent ICE from doing its job.

Ice Makes Nationwide Arrests Of Illegal Immigrants, Murderers, Pedophiles In Holiday Blitz

"He's damn right. Every illegal alien in this nation that's here illegally, [and] crossed the border illegally, which is a crime, needs to be deported," he said.

"That's called the rule of law. If he doesn't like it, call your members of Congress, tell them to change the law."

Homan insisted President Donald Trump's campaign promises to bolster border security and impose mass deportations are a large reason why he holds the Oval Office today, arguing his administration's results have demonstrated his willingness to fulfill those promises.

title: Netanyahu fires back at NYC Mayor Mamdani's vow to enforce 'bogus arrest warrant'
description: Benjamin Netanyahu's office accuses Zohran Mamdani of attacking the Jewish state to distract from the damage his policies have caused New York.
fullText:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office is accusing New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani of attacking the Jewish state to distract from his record in New York after calling Netanyahu a "war criminal."

The prime minister's office fired back Sunday after the Democratic mayor said his administration was exploring whether it could arrest the Israeli leader during an expected visit to the city under the International Criminal Court's warrant.

"The ICC is a kangaroo court that has no jurisdiction over Americans or Israelis," Netanyahu's office wrote in an X post Sunday, amid recent reports he is nearing a trip to the White House to meet with President Donald Trump.

Potential 2028 Dem Contender Unloads On Netanyahu, Admits Us-israel Ties At 'Crossroads'

"Its bogus arrest warrant against Prime Minister Netanyahu was issued by a disgraced former ICC Prosecutor, Karim Khan, a few days before allegations of sexual misconduct against him became public," Netanyahu's office said. "It was a clear attempt by Khan to divert public attention and seek protection from scrutiny."

Mamdani is following the same playbook, the office added.

"Instead of backing Khan's criminal behaviour, Mr. Mamdani should focus on fixing the damage his policies have caused New York," the post concluded. "Like Karim Khan, Mamdani appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East."

Mamdani Sparks Fierce Backlash For World Cup Stunt 'Palling Around' With Rikers Island Convicts

Israel vowed to ignore the ICC warrant while it roots Hamas, "a genocidal terrorist organization ," out of Gaza.

"Under Prime Minister Netanyahu's leadership, Israel has taken unprecedented wartime measures to minimize harm to civilians while confronting Hamas, a genocidal terrorist organization that uses Palestinians as human shields and deliberately targets innocent Israeli civilians," the office's post read.

Former Secretary of State Mike Pompeo told Fox News' "Fox & Friends Weekend" earlier Sunday that Mamdani is ostensibly hyperbolizing for "political theater."

"This is a communist, antisemitic mayor trying to make news for his own audience," Pompeo said. "There's nothing more than that. This is no chance he's going to arrest Prime Minister Netanyahu when he comes to New York in September for the U.N. General Assembly.

"It's just simply not going to happen, and he knows it."

Even some Democrats have rejected Mamdani's stance, warning it could embolden terrorists at home and abroad.

"This isn't a college protest," former Democrat Mayor Eric Adams wrote on X. "This is the City of New York."

"Threatening to arrest the democratically elected leader of one of America's closest allies during U.N. General Assembly week isn't bold. It's reckless," Adams continued. "American diplomats, service members, and officials are stationed in dangerous places around the world. Irresponsible rhetoric like this doesn't stay in New York. It can put Americans at greater risk abroad. Leadership means understanding that your words have consequences. Reckless political theater can have very real-world costs."

Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., has long broken with Democrats on becoming the "anti-Israel party," telling the "clown" Mamdani to "sit down."

"It's such a tough guy to say that kind of thing," Fetterman told Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures." "He has no way to do that, of course. Obviously, America is not even part of that corrupt court. And so he's just a clown to even say that. So he won't try that. Just sit down and focus on other the problems that they have in New York. That's really not your purview. You and I know that. Sit down."

title: LA sports figure Jeff Pearlman admits Angels have helped him ‘rediscover his love for baseball’
description: It’s been a long and exhausting season for the Angels, but it appears the team has helped at least one fan rediscover his love for baseball. New York Times award-winning...
fullText:
It’s been a long and exhausting season for the Angels, but it appears the team has helped at least one fan rediscover his love for baseball.

New York Times award-winning sports author Jeff Pearlman released a video on TikTok, revealing the Angels have rekindled his passion for the sport.

“In the last [month] I’ve gone to probably like five games, three of the five just by myself, and you may think, ‘What kind of loser goes to a baseball game by himself?’ It’s [the] best,” Pearlman said during the TikTok video. “I buy a ticket really high up. … I get free parking because I park offsite, and I just love it.”

“I love the feel of the ballpark, I love the look of the ballpark, I love the green, I love the national anthem, the seventh-inning stretch, I love that the Angels always do these really cheesy giveaways.”

Despite the Angels having a poor record (39-61), Pearlman said he enjoys being able to watch baseball at an affordable price and called it the “cheapest, best bang for your buck ever.”

Pearlman went on to compare how cheap the Angels tickets are compared with Dodgers tickets. He said he’s happy not having to spend $40 to park and spending over $100 for a ticket.

Pearlman is a New York Times best-selling author who writes about football, baseball and basketball. He also wrote a biography on Tupac Shakur.

Some notable books Pearlman has written about LA sports in his career include “Showtime: Magic, Kareem, Riley, and the Los Angeles Lakers Dynasty of the 1980s,” and “Three-Ring Circus: Kobe, Shaq, Phil, and the Crazy Years of the Lakers Dynasty.”

Now attending Angels games for the experience to watch baseball in its purest form, Pearlman said he’s been taking photos with fans in attendance and leaving free copies of his books across the stadium. He enjoys getting a cold brew coffee from the Rise Espresso Bar in the stadium that includes cold foam and Cocoa Puffs.

The 2026 season has been another tough one for Angels fans, and the franchise might lose 100 games in a season for the first time.

Fans at Angel Stadium have been voicing their frustrations toward ownership this year. Earlier this season, they organized protests toward owner Arte Moreno, urging him to sell the team due to another losing season.

The protests have also been happening inside the stadium as fans have been participating in the Tarps Off movement, which has included regular chants of “Sell the team,” echoing from the right field seats.

Pearlman admitted he has mixed feelings about Tarps Off but called it “entertaining.”

“My new happy place is going to Angels games, parking for free, knowing I have a $5 ticket, watching the game, presuming the Angels are probably going to lose,” Pearlman said. “Just relishing in the moment.”

title: Second death reported in Legionnaires’ disease outbreak in New York City
description: A second person has died in connection with an outbreak of Legionnaires’ disease in New York, the New York City Health Department announced Saturday.  The outbreak on Manhattan’s Upper East Side has i...
fullText:
NEW YORK (AP) — A second person has died in connection with an outbreak of Legionnaires' disease in New York, the New York City Health Department announced Saturday.

The outbreak on Manhattan's Upper East Side has infected 72 people, according to health officials. Two people have died. The first death was announced Friday, and the second was announced Saturday.

"We are heartbroken to learn that another New Yorker has lost their life to Legionnaires' disease on the Upper East Side. Our deepest condolences are with their loved ones as they grieve," New York City Health Commissioner Dr. Alister F. Martin said in a statement.

Legionnaires' disease is a form of severe pneumonia caused by a type of bacteria that thrives in warm, stagnant water. Health officials have focused on testing cooling towers that are part of air conditioning systems for large buildings. Property owners have been ordered to drain and disinfect towers where there has been a positive test result.

Health officials did not pinpoint a source for the current outbreak but said there has been a sustained decline in new cases. They said that indicates the source of exposure to Legionella bacteria has likely been eliminated.

Legionnaires' disease is treatable but 10% of people will die due to complications from the illness, according to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Last year, seven people died and more than 100 were sickened during an outbreak in New York's Harlem neighborhood.

title: ‘They’d call us animals’ – World Cup final security slammed amid two-hour queues and ‘total chaos’
description: The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized. Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.
fullText:
The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized.

Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.

The United States Secret Service are said to have taken over security measures for the game because US president Donald Trump will be attending a match for the first time during this summer’s tournament.

Just a few hours before kick-off, fans and journalists launched fierce criticisms of the operation.

Mariana Spinelli, who works for leading Brazilian outlet Globo, took to social media platform X to write: “We’ve been in line for 2 hours for the x-ray to ENTER the stadium. Everything disorganized, a total chaos. If this were in South America, they’d call us animals, third-world country. Since it’s in the US nobody says anything. A nightmare of organization, pathetic.”

Spinelli is clearly of the belief that if such events were to happen in South America, there would be a much higher level of outrage.

Zach Lowy, the chief editor of Breaking The Lines, wrote: “The organization for this World Cup Final has been shambolic. Thousands who’ve been waiting for 3 hours, there’s a lack of security and officials and scanners, a lot of people who are cutting in line, and a lot of people who have gone around in circles without any progress.”

A further complication also arose when soccer fans were warned to attend the stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey up to four hours before the kick off time. Gates opened at 11am EDT.

A glitch in the FIFA security system to scan passes resulted in long queues forming.

There are believed to be two levels of security at the stadium, one of which is run by the Secret Service and the other organized by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

TSA usually oversee airport security. Fans and members of the media attending Sunday’s World Cup final having to pass through airport style scanners before being admitted into the venue.

title: AL Central Team Reportedly Sends Assistant To Keep Eye On Mets' Clay Holmes
description: The New York Mets are expected to be sellers at this year's trade deadline
fullText:
The New York Mets are expected to be sellers at this year's trade deadline, and, surprisingly, the Chicago White Sox could be a buyer interested in one of their players.

"The Chicago White Sox sent Nick Hostelter, their special assistant, to keep taps on Mets starter Clay Holmes, who has begun his rehab assignment after breaking his right fibula on May 15," MLB insider Bob Nightengale noted on Sunday.

Holmes found great success as a reliever for the New York Yankees before eventually signing a three-year, $38 million deal with the New York Mets before the 2025 season. He made 33 appearances, 31 of which were starts, and posted a 3.53 ERA. He had a 2.39 ERA through nine starts this season before he landed on the injured list with a fractured fibula after taking a 111.1 mph comebacker off the leg.

How Holmes performs in his rehab assignment will be important in determining what the Mets can get for him. He has a $12 million player option next season, and the Mets may view him as one of their more valuable trade chips. His arm is relatively fresh, since he spent time on the injured list. He has postseason experience and looked good as a starter for the Mets.

Many teams will be looking for starting pitching at the deadline, and Holmes is an affordable rental who could end up sticking around for another year. He had 45 strikeouts in his nine starts this season and could give any contending rotation a nice boost. Although he is stretched out as a starter, he is a two-time All-Star as a reliever, and teams could look to use him in that role as well. He has had plenty of success in leverage spots out of the bullpen.

title: Trump presence means tight security for World Cup final
description: Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.
fullText:
Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.

Spain face Argentina at the 82,500-seater venue, with kick-off at 15:00 local time (20:00 BST).

There is increased security partly because United States president Donald Trump is attending the final - the only World Cup match he is watching in person.

As a result, the United States Secret Service took over security for the game - leading to long waits for supporters to get into the ground.

BBC Sport understands people were having to queue for about two hours.

But after the long queues, it is understood most fans had no issues scanning tickets and accessing the stadium.

There were two levels of security at the stadium, run by both the Secret Service and Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

The latter is usually responsible for security at airports in the United States, with fans and media having to go through airport security-style scanners.

One member of the media said they, having reached an entrance, were told to go to a different queue as there were no security dogs available to search bags at that gate.

BBC Sport has been told by World Cup volunteers at the ground that the number of people arriving early before the game was much higher than the previous seven matches at the stadium.

Gates opened for supporters at 11:00 local time - four hours before kick-off - with more gates being opened sporadically to ease congestion.

It is not thought there will be any delay to kick-off or any other disruption to the match.

title: More Bad News for Aaron Judge, Yankees
description: The New York Yankees are missing their biggest star right now and that won't change any time soon.
fullText:
The New York Yankees are missing their biggest star in Aaron Judge and made a roster move involving him on Saturday.

On Saturday, the Yankees officially moved Judge to the 60-Day Injured List as he continued to recover from his fractured rib. Now, of course, this doesn't add 60 days to his total amount of time he needs to stay on the Injured List, in large part because he already has missed so much time. But it is yet another example of him not being close to a return.

Judge, himself, gave an update on his rib earlier in the week.

"Some progress, some healing going on, but not fully healed," Judge said. "So we're still waiting on one more doctor to take a look at it, kind of see how we progress forward the next couple of weeks. But definitely a positive sign that we're seeing some healing.”

title: Security separate Benn and Garcia at heated media day
description: Security steps in to stop Conor Benn confronting Ryan Garcia in a heated news conference in New York.
fullText:
Security intervened to stop Conor Benn confronting Ryan Garcia in a heated news conference in New York.

The date for Benn's fight with the WBC welterweight champion was confirmed last week, days after they clashed at the weigh-in for UFC 329.

The pair shouted at each other throughout Saturday's media day, which erupted after Benn told Garcia he would "smack those glasses straight off your face".

The American then yelled an expletive back at Benn, and the British fighter stepped out of his seat before being held back by security as he tried to get to Garcia.

Benn is now promoted by Zuffa Boxing, which is backed by UFC chief Dana White and Saudi Arabian capital.

Benn, 29, had earlier said Garcia is "like a big child, a spoiled brat".

"He throws his toys out of the pram on social media, throwing little tantrums," he added.

"I'm not too concerned about this little influencer. He ain't a fighter. He can say whatever he wants, it ain't going to make a difference come 12 September."

Benn then pointed to himself and said: "12-round war proven. I don't quit, I don't stay down on one knee," referring to Garcia's loss to Gervonta Davis in 2023.

Garcia, 27, responded by saying Benn's "not gonna make it to 12, he's not good enough".

"You think I'm going to let a guy from the UK come here and beat me and take my belt? You've lost your mind," Garcia added.

Born and raised in California, Garcia has Mexican heritage and often carries both an American and Mexican flag into the ring.

"Wait till I smack you up and make you quit," Benn added. "You have no heart. You're not Mexican, bro."

Benn, who spent 12 years of his childhood in Mallorca, Spain, then shouted at Garcia in Spanish while grabbing his crotch.

Garcia later said he would stop Benn inside five rounds before the stage was cleared for a face-off between the fighters.

Benn's father, former world champion Nigel Benn, was standing behind him as he repeatedly shouted "you quit" and told Garcia "you ain't got no heart. I'll test it, I'll test that chin too".

Garcia replied by saying: "This about to be easy work. You not good enough to beat me. Your dad's gonna stop the fight. I promise you."

title: Knicks Receive Final Answer on Promising Free Agent After Mavericks Decision
description: The Knicks’ latest attempt to add a young center ended quickly, leaving New York back at the drawing board after multiple misses. The post Knicks Receive Final Answer on Promising Free Agent After Mavericks Decision appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
The New York Knicks’ search for a young third-string center is back where it started.

The Dallas Mavericks matched New York’s two-year, $4.7 million offer sheet for restricted free agent Moussa Cissé on Saturday night, keeping the 23-year-old center in Dallas and ending the Knicks’ latest attempt to replenish their frontcourt depth.

ESPN’s Shams Charania reported the decision, citing Cissé’s agents, Yann Balikouzou and Kevin Bradbury of LIFT Sports Management. Dallas matched the offer well before Monday’s 48-hour deadline.

For the Knicks, the disappointment extends beyond losing one promising prospect.

Cissé was the latest in a string of young centers New York pursued after Ariel Hukporti left for the Philadelphia 76ers in free agency. With Karl-Anthony Towns established as the starter and Andre Drummond expected to serve as the primary backup, the Knicks still need a developmental big man behind them.

Dallas’ decision sends the defending NBA champions back to the drawing board.

The Knicks signed Cissé to the offer sheet earlier Saturday, structuring a veteran minimum deal with only half of his first-year salary guaranteed and a non-guaranteed second season.

The modest commitment gave New York a low-risk opportunity to add a 6-foot-11 rim protector with developmental upside.

Rather than allow Cissé to leave on an inexpensive contract, the Mavericks exercised their matching rights almost immediately. The decision ensured that Cissé would remain with the organization that gave him his first NBA opportunity.

It also validated New York’s evaluation.

Former Brooklyn Nets assistant general manager Bobby Marks described the offer sheet as an “under-the-radar” move after it was first reported.

NBA analyst Nate Duncan wrote, “Love it!” and argued that more teams should use lower-cost offer sheets to pursue restricted free agents with upside.

Basketball analyst David Lee praised the roster-building concept behind the move.

“The Knicks are trying to replicate Mitchell Robinson in the aggregate,” Lee wrote.

New York ultimately came away empty-handed, but Dallas’ quick response showed the Knicks were not alone in believing Cissé was worth developing.

Cissé was not the first young big man on the Knicks’ radar.

NBA insider Jake Fischer previously reported that New York explored trades for New Orleans Pelicans center Yves Missi and Charlotte Hornets center Moussa Diabate.

Neither pursuit produced a deal.

Missi, a 2024 first-round pick, emerged as an athletic rim-running center during his rookie season with New Orleans. Diabate developed into a productive rebounder and interior defender in Charlotte.

Both fit the profile the Knicks appear to be targeting: a young, mobile center who can protect the rim, rebound and develop behind Towns and Drummond without requiring a prominent role immediately.

After those trade attempts failed, New York pivoted to restricted free agency and tried to pry Cissé away from Dallas.

That avenue has now closed as well.

The Knicks entered the offseason with greater frontcourt turnover than expected.

Mitchell Robinson left for the Boston Celtics. Hukporti then departed for Philadelphia in free agency, leaving New York with only Towns as the center on the roster.

They signed two-time center Andre Drummond for the veteran minimum, but needed another big man as insurance behind him and Towns.

That is the role Cissé would have filled.

He went undrafted after playing collegiately at Memphis and Oklahoma State, but earned an opportunity with Dallas through his length, athleticism and shot-blocking ability.

His most productive NBA performance came against the Knicks, when he recorded 15 points, nine rebounds and four blocks.

The performance offered a glimpse of why New York viewed him as more than a routine end-of-roster signing.

The Knicks remain strong at the top of their center rotation.

Towns is coming off an All-NBA season and a championship run, while Drummond provides size, rebounding and experience behind him.

The concern is what comes next.

New York still lacks a young third center who could absorb limited regular-season minutes, provide injury insurance and potentially develop into a larger role.

The Knicks have already explored three recognizable options in Missi, Diabate and Cissé. All three pursuits have ended without a deal.

That leaves president Leon Rose and the front office searching again, whether through another trade, a different restricted free agent, the waiver wire or an eventual training-camp competition.

Dallas gave the Knicks their final answer on Cissé.

Now New York must find another one.
